cards.text: Types defined by programmers are called {{c1::program-defined types}} (also {{c2::user-defined types}}).

cards.front: What are the two main categories of types in C++?
cards.back:
• Fundamental types (int, double, char, etc.)
  • Program-defined types (enums, structs, classes)

cards.text: The simplest program-defined type is the {{c1::enumerated type}} using keyword {{c2::enum}}.

cards.front: What is the difference between an enumeration and an enumerator?
cards.back:
Enumeration: The type itself (e.g., Color)
Enumerator: Named constant within it (e.g., red, green)

cards.text:
To define a struct: {{c1::struct}} Employee {{c2::{
  int id;
  double wage;
};}}}

cards.front: What is the main difference between struct and class keywords in C++?
cards.back:
Default member access level:
  • struct: public by default
  • class: private by default

cards.explanation:
Create custom types when:
  • Modeling real-world entities with multiple properties
  • Grouping related constants
  • Needing better type safety than built-ins
  • Representing domain-specific concepts
examples: struct Point { int x; int y; };

cards.question: What does this define?
cards.answer: An enumerated type named Color with three enumerators: red, green, and blue.
cards.explanation: Enumerations group related named constants under a type name.

cards.text: Members of a struct are accessed using the {{c1::member selection operator}} ({{c2::.}}).
In C++, {{c1::enum}} defines an unscoped enumeration while {{c2::enum class}} defines a scoped enumeration.
The {{c1::struct}} keyword defines a class with {{c2::public}} default access, while {{c3::class}} has {{c4::private}} default access.
C++ has two type categories: {{c1::fundamental types}} (built-in like int) and {{c2::program-defined types}} (user-created).
In C++, {{c1::enum}} defines an unscoped enumeration while {{c2::enum class}} defines a scoped enumeration.
The {{c1::struct}} keyword defines a class with {{c2::public}} default access, while {{c3::class}} has {{c4::private}} default access.
